title: Deaths due to unsafe sex
subtitle: Estimated annual number of deaths attributed to [unsafe sex](#dod:unsafe_sex). Unsafe sex is defined as the risk of disease due to sexual transmission. Unsafe sex is an important risk factor in cervical cancer, HIV and sexually-transmitted infections.
caption:
Deaths due to unsafe sex, 2023
Estimated annual number of deaths attributed to unsafe sex. Unsafe sex is defined as the risk of disease due to sexual transmission. Unsafe sex is an important risk factor in cervical cancer, HIV and sexually-transmitted infections.

The choropleth map displays the estimated annual number of deaths attributed to unsafe sex across the globe for the year 2023, utilizing a color scale that ranges from light beige for zero deaths to deep maroon for values exceeding 300,000. The visualization reveals a distinct geographic concentration of mortality, with the highest burdens located in southern Africa and South Asia. South Africa and India appear in the darkest maroon shade, indicating that both nations recorded more than 300,000 deaths, representing the maximum values visible on the map. China and the Russian Federation are shaded in a medium-dark orange, placing them in the band between 30,000 and 100,000 deaths. Across the African continent, a broad swath of countries including Tanzania, Mozambique, and Zimbabwe fall into the 10,000 to 30,000 range, while nations in North Africa and the Middle East generally show lower counts in the 1,000 to 3,000 band. In the Americas, Brazil and the United States are visible in the 10,000 to 30,000 category, whereas Canada and most of South America sit in the lighter 1,000 to 3,000 range. Western Europe, Australia, and Greenland are colored in the lightest beige, corresponding to the lowest end of the scale near zero to 1,000 deaths. The data shows a clear disparity where the highest mortality figures cluster in specific high-population regions and southern Africa, while vast areas of the Americas, Europe, and Oceania record significantly lower totals. The most intense concentration of deaths occurs in South Africa and India, where the counts exceed the 300,000 threshold.
